narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 43.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 2—well below the typical student's 5.71—and your Year 3 decile of 2, compared to an average of 5.53, showing consistently lower performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 35 also fell short of the dataset average of 36.7, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was slightly under the 3.21 norm, and your middle family income bracket aligns with about average for applicants. As a White male planning full-time enrollment, your demographics match the majority in our dataset—White students make up 83.8%, males 56.4%, and full-time 92.7%—so they didn't stand out unusually.

In assessing your profile, the model weighed your Year 3 decile as the most influential factor, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure because it signals weaker late-stage academic momentum compared to most students. Your Year 1 decile came in second, reinforcing that early struggles carried significant weight in the model's view. Next was your LSAT score, which had a notable pull toward failure risk since it lagged behind typical successful applicants. Your family income followed closely, with the middle bracket slightly tipping the scales unfavorably in this context, then your undergraduate GPA, which being below average added to the concern.

Less influential but still contributing were your racial background, gender, and full-time status, all nudging the prediction modestly toward failure risk relative to dataset patterns. Overall, these factors combined to make your predicted bar passage less certain than for students with stronger academic deciles and test scores. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above 37, as that's a realistic step that directly addresses one of the top influences. Strengthening your study habits or seeking tutoring could help demonstrate better performance in any graduate-level work, countering those low deciles. Building a stronger narrative around your middle-income background through scholarships or leadership might also help in reapplying, turning potential weaknesses into stories of resilience. With targeted improvements, you could shift closer to the profiles of students who pass the bar on their first attempt. Keep pushing—you've got this potential.
